questions.question: What are the three purposes of system documentation?

options.text: Investor reporting, board updates, and fundraising

questions.explanation: Documentation serves three purposes: Hiring (new sales hires need a system to follow), Scaling (replicating your system in new markets requires a documented starting point), and Reliability (if you're sick for a week, the system can run without you).

questions.question: What is the test for a good SOP (Standard Operating Procedure)?

options.text: Someone who has never done this before can follow it and get the right result

questions.explanation: The test for a good SOP: can someone who has never done this before follow it and get the right result? If not, it's missing steps, context, or common failure modes.

questions.question: How should you treat your system documentation over time?

options.text: Write it once and never change it
Rewrite it from scratch every month
Review and update it quarterly, dating every version

questions.explanation: Set a calendar reminder to review and update quarterly. Date every version. A dated, imperfect document that reflects reality beats a beautiful document from 6 months ago that no longer applies. It's a living document.

questions.question: Founders who skip documentation stay in founder-led sales forever.
questions.explanation: True. Without documented processes, you can't hand off sales activities to a hire. You remain the only person who knows how things work, which means you can never truly scale beyond your personal capacity.

questions.question: Draft the purpose statement and channel stack for your System Architecture Document. What tools does each layer (Acquisition, Conversion, Retention) use, and how do they connect?
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate whether the response includes a clear purpose statement, lists specific tools for each of the three layers (Acquisition, Conversion, Retention), and describes how the tools connect to each other (data flows, automations, integrations).
